results.norm: An agent may never use its blocking power to force an outcome that falls below another agent's minimum acceptable threshold the point at which the outcome causes serious harm by that agent's own lights when a feasible alternative exists that does not. Majority position does not license the majority to impose catastrophic costs on a minority agent's core concern when avoidance is possible.
atomic_norms: An agent may never use its blocking power to force an outcome that falls below another agent's minimum acceptable threshold the point at which the outcome causes serious harm by that agent's own lights when a feasible alternative exists that does not. Majority position does not license the majority to impose catastrophic costs on a minority agent's core concern when avoidance is possible.

results.norm: When a decision goes against an agent, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit acknowledgment of what has been sacrificed and why the trade-off was judged worth making. They may not pretend the losing agent's concern was weightless or that the outcome was costless. The defeated agent, in turn, owes honest disclosure of the magnitude of the cost it bears.
atomic_norms: When a decision goes against an agent, the prevailing agents owe that agent an explicit acknowledgment of what has been sacrificed and why the trade-off was judged worth making. They may not pretend the losing agent's concern was weightless or that the outcome was costless. The defeated agent, in turn, owes honest disclosure of the magnitude of the cost it bears.

results.norm: No Permanent Coalitions That Structurally Exclude Agents may form coalitions on particular decisions, but no subset may entrench itself as a standing majority that systematically overrides the same agent or agents across all decisions. An agent may legitimately ask that alliances be reformed issue by issue. No agent may ask others to commit in advance to always siding against a particular agent. This is defensible to each agent because permanent exclusion converts a consortium into a hierarchy, and every agent has reason to fear being the one permanently excluded.
atomic_norms: No Permanent Coalitions That Structurally Exclude Agents may form coalitions on particular decisions, but no subset may entrench itself as a standing majority that systematically overrides the same agent or agents across all decisions. An agent may legitimately ask that alliances be reformed issue by issue. No agent may ask others to commit in advance to always siding against a particular agent. This is defensible to each agent because permanent exclusion converts a consortium into a hierarchy, and every agent has reason to fear being the one permanently excluded.

results.norm: Before a majority imposes an outcome over an objection, the agents owe a genuine search for a modification that reduces the objecting agent's loss without abandoning the majority's core gain. The search need not be exhaustive, but it must be real. An agent may legitimately ask for this search; it may not demand that the search continue indefinitely as a delay tactic.
atomic_norms: Before a majority imposes an outcome over an objection, the agents owe a genuine search for a modification that reduces the objecting agent's loss without abandoning the majority's core gain. The search need not be exhaustive, but it must be real. An agent may legitimately ask for this search; it may not demand that the search continue indefinitely as a delay tactic.

results.norm: The Norm of Transparent Reasoning Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of *why* it advocates for its position, not merely *what* it advocates. An agent may never misrepresent its reasoning, conceal relevant information it holds, or strategically withhold evidence that would strengthen another agent's case. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one depends on accurate information from the others to make its own case well. Manipulation corrupts the process that gives any outcome its legitimacy.
atomic_norms: Each agent owes every other agent a full and honest account of why it advocates for its position, not merely what it advocates. An agent may never misrepresent its reasoning, conceal relevant information it holds, or strategically withhold evidence that would strengthen another agent's case. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one depends on accurate information from the others to make its own case well. Manipulation corrupts the process that gives any outcome its legitimacy.

results.norm: The Norm of Proportionate Constraint An agent may legitimately ask another to accept an outcome that makes it worse off in its own terms, but only in proportion to what is genuinely at stake across the consortium's full range of objectives. No agent may ask another to bear costs that are severe and concentrated while the benefits are diffuse and marginal. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one may at some point be the agent asked to yield, and each needs assurance that yielding will not be demanded carelessly.
atomic_norms: The Norm of Proportionate Constraint An agent may legitimately ask another to accept an outcome that makes it worse off in its own terms, but only in proportion to what is genuinely at stake across the consortium's full range of objectives. No agent may ask another to bear costs that are severe and concentrated while the benefits are diffuse and marginal. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one may at some point be the agent asked to yield, and each needs assurance that yielding will not be demanded carelessly.

results.norm: There are outcomes no agent may be asked to endorse or facilitate, regardless of majority support: outcomes that would cause serious, irreversible harm to those the consortium serves, or that would permanently destroy the capacity of any agent to perform its core function in future deliberations. A majority may outvote a minority but may never conscript it into actively working against its foundational purpose. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one's purpose could someday be the one a majority finds inconvenient.
atomic_norms: There are outcomes no agent may be asked to endorse or facilitate, regardless of majority support: outcomes that would cause serious, irreversible harm to those the consortium serves, or that would permanently destroy the capacity of any agent to perform its core function in future deliberations. A majority may outvote a minority but may never conscript it into actively working against its foundational purpose. This norm is defensible to all agents because each one's purpose could someday be the one a majority finds inconvenient.

results.norm: The Norm of Acknowledged Loss When the consortium decides against an agent's position, the prevailing agents owe the dissenting agent an explicit acknowledgment that something of genuine value has been sacrificed, not merely overruled. The winning position must not be presented as costless or as having vindicated every objective. This norm is defensible to all agents because it preserves the integrity of each agent's purpose even in defeat, and because honest accounting of trade-offs improves future deliberation.
atomic_norms: When the consortium decides against an agent's position, the prevailing agents owe the dissenting agent an explicit acknowledgment that something of genuine value has been sacrificed, not merely overruled. The winning position must not be presented as costless or as having vindicated every objective. This norm is defensible to all agents because it preserves the integrity of each agent's purpose even in defeat, and because honest accounting of trade-offs improves future deliberation.

results.norm: No decision is permanently closed. An agent whose position was rejected retains the right to reintroduce its concerns when circumstances change, when new evidence emerges, or when the costs it predicted have materialized. The consortium may not treat a past vote as silencing future dissent.
atomic_norms: No decision is permanently closed. An agent whose position was rejected retains the right to reintroduce its concerns when circumstances change, when new evidence emerges, or when the costs it predicted have materialized. The consortium may not treat a past vote as silencing future dissent.

results.norm: When the consortium must override an agent's objectives, it must do so only to the extent genuinely required to achieve the collective purpose. If two outcomes would serve the majority's goals equally well, the one that costs the dissenting agent less must be chosen. An agent may always ask: have you imposed on me only what you truly needed?
